Hey Jose,

You mentioned that a lot of what I showed in the first video was actually your neighbour's side of the roof. You're right to flag that, and it's a fair point. So I went back through the photos and pulled out only your half of the duplex, 16 Whisperwood. Everything in this report, every photo and every mark, is on your portion of the roof and nothing else.

Here is exactly what I'm seeing on your side, grouped by the type of issue so it's easy to follow. The colored marks on the photos are mine. There's a quick key for them right below.

- Mackenzie

How to read the marks

I marked the photos in two colors as I went over your side. Here's what each one means.

Red: water likely getting in now

Where I marked red, water is most likely already getting in. These are the spots letting the weather through as we speak.

Yellow: trouble area, soon

Yellow is a trouble spot. Not leaking yet, but it's heading that way and won't hold much longer.

What I found on your side

Four things stood out across your half of the roof, front and back.

Blown-off and missing shingles, front and back

There are shingles gone and knocked out of place on both slopes. On a roof this age there's no underlayment under the shingles, so a blow-off leaves the bare wood deck exposed (most likely OSB) and lines the seams up. That's a direct path for water into the deck, and likely into the attic below.

Water getting in now

Exposed and tarred-over nail heads

A whole run of nail heads is sitting exposed on the surface, a lot of them tarred over by hand to try to seal them. Some have already torn through and the nails are breaking free. Every one of those is a direct hole into the roof.

Water getting in nowTrouble soon

Cracked shingles and broken creases

A number of shingles have broken creases right across them, so they're already split. They're hanging on by a thread. Once a shingle is cracked through like that it can't shed water the way it's meant to.

Trouble soon

Repeated patching over the years

Large areas of your side have been patched before, with mismatched shingles swapped in over time. That tells me this roof has been giving trouble for a while and has been kept alive with repairs rather than fixed at the source.

Trouble soon

The photos

Your side of the roof, with my marks on it. The mismatched grey patches are older repairs; the circled spots are the failures called out above.

Your repair and spray option

There's a middle path before a full replacement. We fix what's failing, hand-seal the rest, and rejuvenate your shingles, all in one day on site. This covers your unit only. Your neighbour's side is separate and isn't part of your cost.

Repair + Revive spray, your side

$4,500 + HST

$5,175 all-in

Financing available

About $108/month over 60 months

About $165/month over 36 months

Through FinanceIt, on approved credit. No penalty for paying it off early.

  • Patch the damaged areas on the back upper slope
  • Reset and secure the displaced shingles (front dormer and lower back)
  • Hand-seal every exposed tab and nail head across your side
  • A full Revive rejuvenation spray over your roof once the repairs are done
  • A 10-year Revive warranty on the spray, backed by Plus Ultra Roofing
  • One day on site, cleanup included

Straight talk: with the repairs done first, the spray is covered by its full 10-year warranty, so this is a real option, not a band-aid. The one honest caveat is the age of the shingles underneath, which is why I still lean toward replacement below.

My honest take

If it were mine, I'd lean toward replacing it.

I'll give it to you straight. The repair and spray is a real option, and with everything fixed first it carries the full 10-year Revive warranty. At $4,500 it stops the active problems and keeps the spend down. The honest tradeoff is the shingles underneath are aged, so even warranted, you're putting good money into a roof that's later in its life. By the time you total the repairs and the spray, you're at roughly 60 to 65 percent of what replacing your side would cost.

Replacing your side resets the clock, comes with the full workmanship warranty, and you wouldn't have to think about it again. It's the better long-term value if the budget is there. I measured the first number off the whole duplex by mistake, so let me get you the exact figure for just your half if you want to set them side by side.

Either way is honest work and there's no wrong answer here. Have a look, then reply or give me a call and we'll go from there.

Your options Repair + spray $5,175 all-in, or replace your side (quote on request)
